coredata.dc:title: Nonmonotonic temperature dependence of fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in a glycerol solution
coredata.dc:creator: Starukhin, A.N.
Nelson, D.K.
Eurov, D.A.
Kurdyukov, D.A.
Grudinkin, S.A.
coredata.dc:description: The effect of temperature on the fluorescence of carbon dot colloidal solution in glycerol has been studied. The dots were prepared by pyrolysis of a Nile Red laser dye using mesoporous silica as a template. It is established that the temperature dependence of the intensity of dominant emission peak is nontrivial: as the temperature of the solution rises, the initial quenching of the fluorescence is then replaced by its enhancement to the initial value. A three-level model of electronic states is considered, within which the observed temperature dependence of fluorescence is quantitatively described and the energies of nonradiative deactivation and activation of the emissive state are estimated. The nature of the electronic state responsible for the nonradiative activation of the emissive state is discussed. The possible influence of hydrogen bonds in the solute-solvent system on the temperature behavior of the CD fluorescence is also considered. A pronounced solvatochromic effect is noted in the fluorescence spectra of Nile Red based carbon dots in different solvents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nile red dye
Fluorescence
Fluorescence quenching/enhancement

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the CD synthesis process (A) and a HRTEM image of the CDs synthesized (B).
FTIR spectra of precursor (Nile Red) (a) and dry carbon dots (b).
Room-temperature absorption spectra of the CD solution (solid line) and NR solution (dashed line) in glycerol. The arrows mark the CD absorption peaks.

Room-temperature normalized emission spectra of carbon dots (CDs) in glycerol and Nile Red (NR) solution in ethanol. hν
exc = 3.061 eV. The dashed lines (1 and 2) represent the result of the decomposition of the CD spectrum into two Gaussian peaks.

Fluorescence spectra of CD solution in glycerol at different temperatures (solid lines). hν
exc = 3.061 eV. The dashed lines represent the result of the decomposition of the spectra into Gaussian peaks.
Dependence of the position of the short-wavelength fluorescence peak on temperature (■). The dashed line is a guide for the eye.
The temperature dependence of the integrated CD fluorescence intensity of short-wavelength peak. The points are experimental data; the solid line is the fit of the experimental points by Eq. (1).
Three-level model used in the analysis for the temperature dependences of the CD fluorescence intensity. Dashed curved lines show thermally induced nonradiative electron transitions.
Room-temperature emission spectra of CDs in ethanol (1), DMSO (2), and glycerol (3). hν exc = 3.061 eV.

The dependence of the intergrated intensity of the 2.45 eV fluorescence band in the spectra of CD solution in DMSO. The points are experimental data; the solid line is the fit of the experimental points by Eq. (2).

head.ce:title: Nonmonotonic temperature dependence of fluorescence intensity of carbon dots in a glycerol solution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of temperature on the fluorescence of carbon dot colloidal solution in glycerol has been studied. The dots were prepared by pyrolysis of a Nile Red laser dye using mesoporous silica as a template. It is established that the temperature dependence of the intensity of dominant emission peak is nontrivial: as the temperature of the solution rises, the initial quenching of the fluorescence is then replaced by its enhancement to the initial value. A three-level model of electronic states is considered, within which the observed temperature dependence of fluorescence is quantitatively described and the energies of nonradiative deactivation and activation of the emissive state are estimated. The nature of the electronic state responsible for the nonradiative activation of the emissive state is discussed. The possible influence of hydrogen bonds in the solute-solvent system on the temperature behavior of the CD fluorescence is also considered. A pronounced solvatochromic effect is noted in the fluorescence spectra of Nile Red based carbon dots in different solvents.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) show considerable promise as new man-made luminophores which have attracted attention due to a good combination of properties such as bright photoluminescence, low toxicity, low cost and relative ease of synthesis. These virtues compare CDs favourably from semiconductor quantum dots. The dots are typically quasispherical nanoparticles (up to 10 nm in size) comprised of amorphous or crystalline cores of graphitic or turbostratic carbon with sp2 carbon sheets [1]. During CD synthesis various functional groups (carbonyl, carboxyl, etc.) become attached to the surface of the graphite core, and, apparently, to a large extent determine the optical properties, chemical activity, degree of hydrophilicity, and other characteristics of CDs [2–6]. The possibility of functionalizing the surface of a nanodot with diverse functional groups opens the way to an intentional change in its properties [7,8]. Along with applications in optoelectronics, storage and conversion devices, catalysis, chemical sensing CDs are of particular interest from the point of view of various biomedical applications [9–13]. The feasibility of all these applications is mainly based on the luminescent properties of the CDs exhibited by them in solutions. Meanwhile the luminescence origin remains the most unclear aspect of the nanoparticles [14]. To elucidate the origin of luminescence, apparently, a comprehensive study of the various properties of the emissive centers (“fluorophores”) is required.

The temperature dependence of nanoparticle emission is an important characteristic of emissive centers, providing information on the peculiarities of energy transfer between the emissive and nonradiative states of the center (or its environment). In particular, the well-known temperature quenching of CD fluorescence (a decrease in its intensity with increasing temperature) is associated with the thermally induced transitions of photoexcited electrons from emissive to higher-lying nonradiative states. However, a decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing temperature is not a general pattern, and the inverse effect of “negative” thermal quenching is observed in a number of semiconductor materials [15–18] and molecular species [19]. Given the frequent comparison of semiconductor and carbon dots, it is of interest to study to what extent the effect of “negative” thermal quenching or, more generally, the nonmonotonic dependence of the emission intensity on temperature is characteristic of carbon dots. This paper presents the results of a study of the temperature dependence of the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots synthesized by pyrolysis of Nile Red and shows that this dependence has a nontrivial character, in which, with an increase in temperature, the portions of rise and fall of the intensity follow each other. The results obtained were quantitatively described within the framework of the 3-level model. The possible influence of hydrogen bonds in the solute-solvent system on the temperature behavior of fluorescence is also considered.

Carbon dots were synthesized in nanopores of silica particles using organic laser dye 9-diethylaminobenzo[a]-phenoxazin-5-one (Nile Red, Sigma-Aldrich) as a precursor containing nitrogen and oxygen atoms which are known to participate in the formation of emissive centers in CDs [20,21]. It is worth noting that Nile Red (NR) belongs to the heterocyclic compounds as well as a wide variety of other organic dyes, for example, rhodamines, even the addition of which to the reaction mixture for the CD synthesis markedly changes the properties (including morphology) of CDs synthesized [22]. Spherical particles of mesoporous silica were prepared by a procedure described elsewhere [23]. The particles have an internal system of cylindrical nanochannels of uniform diameter (it could be varied, if necessary, in the range from 2 to 5 nm). The particles were monodisperse in size with their diameters 500 ± 20 nm, and the pore diameter measured 3.10 ± 0.15 nm. A weighed quantity of mesoporous silica spheres was impregnated with 33% (by volume) Nile Red solution in methanol (99.8%, Acros). The particles were then dried at 50 °C and annealed in air at 330 °C for 2 h. Thermal decomposition of NR resulted in the formation of carbon dots uniformly distributed in the silica spheres. The advantage of the template-assisted method is the possibility of shape and size control by architecture of template, which may result in CDs with higher monodispersity without additional purification procedures [24]. To separate carbon dots from a silica matrix the latter was dissolved in hydrofluoric acid (ACS reagent grade). After the mesoporous silica particles were dissolved, the sediment containing carbon dots was five times ultrasonically redispersed, to remove SiO2 dissolution products, in deionized water (10 MΩ) and then was sedimented by centrifugation. The carbon dot content in concentrated water suspension was determined gravimetrically and found to be 0.06 % wt. (∼0.03% vol.). The carbon dot concentration in suspension was ∼1016 ea/cm−3 (by taking the volume of one carbon dot equal to ∼ 20 nm3). As noted above, NR like rhodamines is a heterocyclic compound with conjugated bonds. There is reason to believe that the morphology of NR-based CDs is similar to that of rhodamine dye-based CDs we synthesized earlier under similar conditions, with fragments of the dye molecules being incorporated into nanodots synthesized [25]. A scheme illustrating the CD synthesis process and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CDs synthesized are depicted in Fig. 1 . Microscopic studies were carried out using transmission electron microscope Jeol JEM-2100 F.

The concentrated suspension of carbon dots in water was used for preparing colloidal solutions of the dots in glycerol (≥99.5%, Sigma-Aldrich). The amount of the added concentrated carbon dot suspension was 100 μL per 1.1 mL of glycerol. Thus, the carbon dot concentration in the solutions was about 1015 ea/cm3. To avoid the aggregation of carbon dots in the glycerol-water mixture its pH value was kept at 10 by adding small amount of ammonia spirit (25 % wt, ≥99.99%) to the solutions (2 μL of 10 M NH3·H2O).

To identify the functional groups, which are constituents of CDs, their FTIR transmission spectra were examined. For this purpose, CDs were applied on a KDB-10 crystalline silicon wafer. IR transmission spectra were measured with Bruker 113v IFS Fourier spectrometer using a DTGS detector in vacuum. The spectral resolution was 4 cm−1. The spectra were obtained from a sample area of 1.25 × 1.25 mm2. UV-near IR absorption spectra of carbon dots were recorded with use of SF2000 spectrophotometer operating in the spectral region of 200–1000 nm.

Fluorescence of CD solutions was excited by CW radiation of laser operating at 405 nm. The excitation densities did not exceed 1 W/cm2. A thin-walled quartz cuvette with the solution was placed in an optical thermostat, which made it possible to maintain the sample temperature in the range 0–180 °C with an accuracy of 0.5 °C.The exciting radiation was incident normally on the cuvette surface, and the emitted light was detected at a right angle to the exciting beam (the right angle geometry). The emission spectra were recorded using grating spectrometers. Taking into account the possibility of photochemical changes in the solution leading to change in its optical properties, in particular, its absorptivity, the light transmission at 405 nm (i.e., the excitation power absorbed by the solution) was continuously monitored during the experiment. An analysis of the temperature dependence of the CD fluorescence anisotropy based on the Perrin equation [26] makes it possible to estimate the size of the dots as 3.5 ± 0.3 nm, which is in good agreement with the results of measuring the CD size by electron microscopy (Fig. 1). This size is typical for the size of carbon dots synthesized in nanopores of mesoporous silica also from other precursors [27].

The FTIR transmission spectra of the dry CDs and starting material (Nile Red) and the interpretation of their main absorption peaks are presented in Fig. 2 . The FTIR spectrum of CDs has a number of prominent peaks (at 3350, 3220, 3070, 1720, 1630, 1510, 1460, 1380, 1310, 1230, 1110 cm−1) which can be ascribed to characteristic absorption bands of OH, C–H, CO, CC, C–C, C–O, C–O–C functional groups. The presence of CC peak indicates that the CDs are made of graphitic structure, whereas the OH, CO, and C–H bending peaks suggest that the CDs have hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl and alkyl functional groups on their surface [28]. The FTIR spectrum of CDs in glycerol solution is difficult to identify because of the strong IR absorption of the relatively large mass of the solvent. Therefore, to study solute-solvent interaction, the spectra of solvent-wetted CDs were investigated. When wetting the CDs, the peak at 1720 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum of dry CDs, associated with the excitation of the carbonyl-group vibrations in the CDs, shifts towards lower energies (by ∼12 cm−1), which one can consider as evidence of formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between the carbon dots and solvent molecules [29].

UV-near IR absorption spectrum of carbon dots under study is depicted in Fig. 3 . Several absorption bands are observed in the spectrum with maxima at λ = 218 nm (hν = 5.687 eV), 264 nm (4.696 eV), 316 nm (3.923 eV), 346 nm (3.583 eV) and 553 nm (2.242 eV). The short wavelength bands below 300 nm are commonly ascribed to a π → π* transition involving aromatic sp2 carbons (aromatic CC bonds), and the structure in the region 300–400 nm is assigned to the absorption of n → π* transition of the CO bonds in carbon cores [30–32]. The longer wavelength absorption is regarded to be due to CO and CN related surface defects [21]. For comparison, Fig. 3 also depicts the absorption spectrum of starting NR in glycerol solution. The UV structure observed in the NR spectrum is to a great extent analogous to that observed in the CD one. The strong absorption band at ∼580 nm (∼2.14 eV) in the NR-dye spectrum, which has a weak counterpart in the CD spectrum, is ascribed to optical transitions involving N atoms in the NR [33]. The presence of absorption bands in this yellow-orange spectral region is known to be also characteristic of N-doped carbon dots [21].

Room-temperature fluorescence spectrum of carbon dot solution in glycerol under optical excitation with wavelength λ
exc = 405 nm (hν
exc = 3.061 eV) is presented in Fig. 4 . As seen in Fig. 4, the fluorescence spectrum consists of at least two broad overlapping bands with maxima at hν
max1 ≈ 2.40 eV (λ
max1 ≈ 515 nm) and hν
max2 ≈ 2.01 eV ((λ
max2 ≈ 615 nm). With good accuracy, the spectrum can be decomposed into two Gaussian-shaped bands (bands 1 and 2, Fig. 4). Based on the spectral position of the bands, the short wavelength emission band 1 can be assigned to radiative transitions involving O-related (carbonyl) emissive states [20], while the long wavelength emission band 2 is commonly assigned to radiative transitions involving N-related states [21]. The latter is confirmed by the immediate proximity of the spectral positions of band 2 and the NR emission band (Fig. 4), which originates from charge transfer from the carbonyl oxygen to the nitrogen of the diethylamino group in NR molecules [34].

The dependence of the integrated intensity of the short-wavelength fluorescence band (considered as a Gaussian peak) on temperature is shown in Fig. 7 . The temperature dependence of the band intensity measured at the band maximum is close to the I(T) dependence shown in Fig. 7, which is understandable taking into account the relatively small thermally induced broadening of the band.

The dependence shows the presence of two opposite tendencies: thermally induced quenching of fluorescence and its enhancement. The first tendency prevails in the region of “low” temperatures (T ≲ 350 K, region I), the second becomes dominant at higher temperatures (region II). Thermal quenching of fluorescence is a well-known effect, usually associated with a thermally induced transition of a system from an emissive state 1 to a nonradiative one 2, separated from the emissive state by an energy barrier of height E
a (Fig. 8 ). An increase in temperature intensifies molecular thermal motion and collisional processes in a molecular system, which promotes the deactivation of excited electronic states through nonradiative vibrational relaxation in the system. On the other hand, in contrast to region I, in region II, an increase in temperature leads to an increase in the fluorescence intensity. The behavior of I(T) in region II corresponds to the temperature behavior of the intensity of radiative transitions in an electron system in which thermally induced electron transitions from an excited long-lived (“dark”) state 3 to a higher-lying emissive state 1 take place. A possible scheme of the electron energy levels corresponding to the above-described processes of thermal deactivation and reactivation of the excited emissive state is shown in Fig. 8.

An analytical description of the temperature dependence of the fluorescence intensity for such a three-level system has been given elsewhere [36]. In the simplest case, temperature induced variations of the fluorescence can be expressed as
(1)
I
( T )
= I
( 0 )
1 + C
exp
(
−
E
e x c
/
k T
)
1 + D
exp
(
−
E a
/
k T
)
where E
exc and E
a are activation energies for thermal excitation and the thermal depletion of the emissive state, respectively. C and D are proportionality factors. Solid line in Fig. 7 represents the result of fitting equation (1) to the experimental data. The fitting procedure was performed using the orthogonal distance regression method. It is evident that the experimental
I
( T )
dependence presented in the figure is well approximated by equation (1). The theoretical curve in Fig. 7 is obtained using the parameters E
exc = 0.46 ± 0.04 eV, E
a = 0.13 ± 0.11 eV. Taking into account the obtained values of E
exc , E
a and the coefficients C and D, at T < 350 K (region I) we have
C
exp
(
−
E
e x c
/
k T
)
≪ 1
and relation (1) can be written as
(2)
I
( T )
≈
I
( 0 )
1 + D
exp
(
−
E a
/
k T
)

Relation (2) corresponds to the situation in which the thermal transfer of electrons from the long-lived state 3 to the emissive state 1 is negligibly small. The coefficient D in relation (2) is the ratio of the radiative lifetime of the emissive state τ
r to its nonradiative lifetime τ nr0 at
T → ∞
(in the case under consideration, D ≈ 2.56∙105). The relation (2) is well known [37] and is widely used to describe the temperature quenching of luminescence in various systems including carbon dots [38]. At T ≈ 350K, the intensities of thermal deactivation (transitions 1 → 2) and activation (transitions 3 → 1) of the emissive state 1 equalize. With a further increase in temperature, the process of thermal activation of electrons from states with energy values lower than those of the emissive state becomes dominant, which manifests itself in an increase in the emission intensity.

The nature of the states responsible for the thermally activated fluorescence (i.e., states 1 and 3 (Fig. 8) is of considerable interest. By analogy with semiconductor crystals [36] and nanodiscs [18], state 3 can correspond to electron traps with the electron energies in between the CD HOMO and LUMO gap. The thermal activation of electrons from these states can promote their transfer to the emissive state 1 and, thereby, enhancement of the emission.

On the other hand, a qualitatively different approach is possible to explain the non-trivial temperature dependence of the fluorescence intensity. As noted above, there is good reason to believe that the emissive centers responsible for fluorescence with hν ≲ 2.5 eV are associated with a CD surface [32]. In our case, this is evidenced, in particular, by the strong dependence of the CD emission spectra on the solvent used. To illustrate the solvatochromic effect [39,40], Fig. 9 depicts the fluorescence spectra of CDs in a number of solvents. Whereas a short-wavelength peak (hν max = 2.40 eV) dominates in the fluorescence spectrum of the CD solution in glycerol, a long-wavelength peak (hν max = 2.01 eV) is a major contributor to the fluorescence spectra of the dots in ethanol and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) (Fig. 9).

Carbonyl oxygen and/or nitrogen, which are part of emissive centers active in green-red region, have the property of a proton acceptor. Taking into account that glycerol is a protic solvent, one can assume that there is an interaction between the carbonyl oxygen of the emissive center (i.e. fluorophore) and the proton-donor solvent, leading to the formation of complexes with hydrogen bonds. This conclusion is in agreement with the FTIR data presented above.

The formation of such complexes may noticeably change the fluorophore properties. So, in case of NR alcoholic solutions, the fluorescence lifetime markedly decreases with the increase of the hydrogen bond donating ability of solvents because vibrations associated with hydrogen bonding are involved in the deactivation process [34]. A similar situation may also take place in a CD solution in glycerol. In this case, an increase in temperature is to lead to breaking the hydrogen bonds [41], which favours an increase in the lifetime of the emissive state, and, accordingly, to an increase in fluorescence intensity. Note that the temperature dependence of the intensity of the 2.45 eV fluorescence band in the spectra of CD solution in DMSO, which is an aprotic solvent, is characterized by a monotonic decrease in intensity with increasing temperature. This dependence is well described by relation (2) with E
a = 0.14 ± 0.02 eV (Fig. 10 ).

By analogy with NR solutions, the thermostimulated breakage of hydrogen bonds is also likely to contribute to the blue shift of the short-wavelength emission peak of the CD solution as the temperature increases (Fig. 6). Indeed, the formation of hydrogen bonds in the solutions of NR [42] and some other dyes [43] results in a red shift of the absorption and fluorescence bands in the spectra of these dyes. Breaking the bonds is expected to have the opposite effect and give rise to the blue shift of the fluorescence. It is worth also noting that the blue shift of the short-wavelength band in the emission spectrum of а CD solution in DMSO when the temperature increases from 298 to 413 K is an order of magnitude smaller than the shift of the similar emission band of а CD solution in glycerol.

It should be noted that the common features in the behavior of the fluorescence spectra of NR and NR-based CDs indirectly evidence incorporating fragments of the precursor molecules into the CDs, as is the case for CDs synthesized from rhodamine dyes [22,25].

Fluorescence spectra of NR based CD solutions are noted for the presence of pronounced structure and for nontrivial temperature behavior. The behavior indicates a nonmonotonic temperature dependence of the emissive state population in conditions of constant excitation, suggesting the presence of processes of both nonradiative deactivation and activation of the emissive state. The considered description of these processes in the framework of the three-level model made it possible to estimate the energies of the corresponding processes. The possible nature of the electronic states responsible for the thermally induced activation of the emissive state is discussed. It was established that the optical absorption spectra of CDs and NR in the UV region were very similar, the differences in their fluorescence spectra in the visible region being more pronounced. The fluorescence spectra of the studied CDs are shown to be noted for a pronounced solvatochromic effect in different solvents, which indicates involving CD surface states in the formation of the fluorescence spectrum of the dots.

A.N. Starukhin: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing. D.K. Nelson: Investigation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. D.A. Eurov: Investigation, Methodology, Validation. D.A. Kurdyukov: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – review & editing. S.A. Grudinkin: Investigation, Methodology, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the RFBR (grant No 20-03-00656). The authors thank D.A. Kirilenko for TEM measurements.
